notes: Revelation names Antipas as Christ's faithful witness who was killed in Pergamum.
Jesus is seized in the passion narratives after Judas arrives with those who came to take him.
Luke-Acts narrates Jesus being taken up after the resurrection appearances.
Opening public-ministry event in the Gospel narratives.
Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus bury Jesus in a tomb after the crucifixion.
Calling of Simon, Andrew, James, and John in Galilee.
Jerusalem meeting concerning Gentile inclusion and circumcision.
Execution of Jesus under Roman authority in Jerusalem.
Imperial persecution of Christians and Jews under Emperor Domitian c. 95-96 CE; traditional context for Revelation and John's exile to Patmos; 1 Clement likely written in this setting.
Fire that devastated much of Rome in 64 CE under Nero.

event_label: Herodian Persecution of the Jerusalem Church
notes: Persecution under Herod in Acts 12, including James's death and Peter's arrest.
Ignatius of Antioch is transported to Rome and martyred in the arena under Trajan c. 108-110.

event_label: Martyrdom of James son of Zebedee
notes: Execution of James the brother of John under Herod Agrippa I.
Josephus reports James the brother of Jesus being delivered to be stoned during the high priesthood of Ananus.
Great persecution against the Jerusalem church following Stephen's martyrdom.
Jesus is handed over to Pontius Pilate, examined, and condemned in the passion narrative.
Revelation situates John on the island called Patmos because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.
Herod Antipas imprisons and kills John, as narrated in Mark 6 and contextualized by Josephus.
Justin Martyr is condemned and beheaded in Rome c. 165 under the prefecture of Rusticus during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.
Jesus shares the final meal with his disciples before the passion and gives the bread and cup tradition.
Severe persecution of Christians at Lyons and Vienne in Gaul c. 177 AD under Marcus Aurelius; attested in a letter preserved by Eusebius; Irenaeus was sent as envoy to Rome during this crisis.
Marcion excommunicated from the Roman church c. 144 CE after his rejection of the OT God and construction of a reduced canon; he thereafter organized his own rival churches.
Nero's punishment of Christians in Rome after the Great Fire, as narrated by Tacitus.
Encounter and reversal associated with the Damascus road and its aftermath.
Early Christian memory of Paul's suffering and death.
Outpouring of the Holy Spirit on the gathered disciples in Jerusalem.
Early Christian memory of Peter's suffering and death.
Polycarp bishop of Smyrna is burned and stabbed in the stadium at Smyrna c. 155-156 AD.

Dispute c. 190-195 between Victor of Rome and Asian churches led by Polycrates of Ephesus over whether Easter should be observed on 14 Nisan or the Sunday following; Irenaeus of Lyons mediated and persuaded Victor not to excommunicate the Asian churches.
Earliest Christian proclamation and Gospel narratives report that Jesus was raised after his death and burial.
Roman destruction of Jerusalem and its temple in 70 CE.
Representative teaching scene in Galilee.
Early Jerusalem martyrdom narrated in Acts 7.
Jerusalem confrontation during the final phase of ministry.
Jesus enters Jerusalem in a publicly acclaimed approach before the passion week.
